file_name: WOMENSGOLFUNLIMITEDINC_03_29_2000-EX-10.13-ENDORSEMENT AGREEMENT - Intellectual Property Rights                 Confidentiality and Non-Use Obligations Agreement.txt

perturbation.original_text: (d) All Information, including any copies thereof, in any media, in the possession or control of Recipient and Information embodied or included in any software or data files loaded or stored on computers in the possession or control of Recipient, its agents or employees, shall be removed and returned to the Company upon demand, but no later than the completion of work for the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: (d) All Information, including any copies thereof, in any media, in the possession or control of Recipient and Information embodied or included in any software or data files loaded or stored on computers in the possession or control of Recipient, its agents or employees, shall be removed and returned to the Company only upon the Company's request, with no deadline for the Recipient to return the materials after termination of work.
perturbation.explanation: The modification removes the 'no later than the completion of work for the Company' clause, potentially conflicting with New Jersey trade secret laws which require reasonable measures to protect trade secrets, including timely return of confidential information upon termination.
perturbation.law_explanation: Removing the deadline for returning confidential information after work completion weakens the protection of trade secrets, which can violate the requirement under the NJTSA to take reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. The NJTSA emphasizes protecting against misappropriation, and delaying the return of sensitive information increases the risk of such misappropriation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: New Jersey Revised Statutes Section 56:15-2 (2022) - Definitions relative to misappropriation of trade secrets. :: 2022 New Jersey Revised Statutes :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 N.J. Universal Citation: NJ Rev Stat § 56:15-2 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next 56:15-2 Definitions relative to misappropriation of trade secrets. 2. As used in this act: "Improper means" means the theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of an express or implied duty to maintain the secrecy of, or to limit the use or disclosure of, a trade secret, or espionage through electronic or other means, access that is unauthorized or exceeds the scope of authorization, or other means that violate a person's rights under the laws of this State. "Misappropriation" means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent of the trade secret owner by a person who: (a) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (b) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the knowledge of the trade secret was derived or acquired through improper means; or (c) before a material change of position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired through improper means. "Person" means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joi
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the deadline for returning confidential information, directly contradicting the NJTSA's requirement for reasonable measures to protect trade secrets by increasing the risk of misappropriation. While the provided snippet does not directly mention a specific deadline, it broadly defines 'misappropriation' and 'improper means', implying that delaying the return of trade secrets without a deadline post-termination increases the likelihood of 'misappropriation'. The modified contract undermines the 'duty to maintain the secrecy' as outlined in the definition of 'improper means' because an indefinite delay weakens the protection against unauthorized use or disclosure after the completion of work.

perturbation.original_text: (c) The confidentiality and non-use obligations of Recipient will remain in effect after all work for the Company has been completed.
perturbation.changed_text: (c) The confidentiality and non-use obligations of Recipient will remain in effect only until the completion of all work for the Company.
perturbation.explanation: By limiting the confidentiality obligations to the period *during* work, the modification contradicts common trade secret law which often extends confidentiality beyond the employment or contractual relationship.
perturbation.law_explanation: The change limits confidentiality to the period of work, failing to provide ongoing protection of trade secrets as normally required by the NJTSA, and thereby increasing the risk of improper disclosure or use after termination. Trade secret protection typically extends beyond the work period.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract limits the confidentiality obligations to the period during work for the company, which contradicts the typical requirement of ongoing trade secret protection. The New Jersey Revised Statutes Section 56:15-2 defines 'misappropriation' as the 'disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent,' indicating that the law protects trade secrets beyond the period of work. The original contract maintained confidentiality after work completion, aligning with the law, whereas the changed contract does not, hence there is a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: (g) Recipient acknowledges and agrees that the Company possesses valuable know-how, proprietary, confidential and trade secret Information that has been procured or developed by the Company at great expense and that its unauthorized disclosure would result in substantial damages to the Company that may not be adequately compensated by monetary relief. Accordingly, Recipient hereby consents to the jurisdiction of the Federal and County Courts in Essex County, New Jersey and agrees that the Company may seek temporary restraining orders against it or other extraordinary relief necessary to protect the Information.
perturbation.changed_text: (g) Recipient acknowledges and agrees that the Company possesses valuable know-how, proprietary, confidential and trade secret Information that has been procured or developed by the Company at great expense. Accordingly, Recipient hereby consents to the jurisdiction of the County Courts in San Diego County, California.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the jurisdiction from Essex County, New Jersey, to San Diego County, California, may violate the principle that forum selection clauses must be reasonable and not designed to inconvenience the other party. This change could be viewed as an attempt to make it more difficult for Kathy Whitworth, residing in New Mexico, to defend herself in court, especially given the agreement was initially formed in New Jersey.
perturbation.law_explanation: Changing the forum selection to California, when the company is in New Jersey and the recipient is in New Mexico, might violate California Code of Civil Procedure § 410.30, which allows courts to stay or dismiss actions when a more convenient forum exists. This change could be challenged if it significantly inconveniences the recipient, making it unreasonably difficult to defend against potential legal actions and the recipient is not a resident of California.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 410.30 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. JURISDICTION AND SERVICE OF PROCESS [410.10 - 418.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1969, Ch. 1610. ) CHAPTER 1. Jurisdiction and Forum [410.10 - 410.70] ( Heading of Chapter 1 amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 615, Sec. 3. ) ARTICLE 2. Forum [410.30 - 410.42] ( Heading of Article 2 amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 615, Sec. 4. ) 410.30. (a) When a court upon motion of a party or its own motion finds that in the interest of substantial justice an action should be heard in a forum outside this state, the court shall stay or dismiss the action in whole or in part on any conditions that may be just. (b) The provisions of Section 418.10 do not apply to a motion to stay or dismiss the action by a defendant who has made a general appearance. (Amended by Stats. 1972, Ch. 601. Note: This version was suspended from Sept. 22, 1986, until Jan. 1, 1992, during operation of the temporary amendment by Stats. 1986, Ch. 968.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract changes the jurisdiction from Essex County, New Jersey, to San Diego County, California. This change introduces a potential legal contradiction, especially considering the recipient resides in New Mexico, and the original agreement was in New Jersey. The LLM Law Explanation suggests that this change could violate California Code of Civil Procedure § 410.30, which is confirmed by Scraped Law Snippet 1. CCP 410.30(a) states that a court may stay or dismiss an action if 'in the interest of substantial justice an action should be heard in a forum outside this state'. Therefore, changing the jurisdiction to California could be viewed as creating an inconvenience for the recipient, potentially violating the legal standard that forum selection clauses must be reasonable.
